title: Add unknown-unknown category to skill security audit
description: The skill security audit in soul_manager.py checks for four known attack patterns (suspicious network requests, dangerous filesystem ops, obfuscated code, privilege escalation). By the reading note's logic, this is exactly the failure mode: we've defined a threat model using known attacks as the coordinate system. A novel attack vector outside those four categories passes the audit silently. Add a fifth structural-anomaly check that flags skills deviating from known-good baselines — not because they match a known-bad pattern, but because they don't match any known-good pattern.
rationale: The reading note's core claim: '把我们已经找到的机制误认为这个功能的机制' — the circadian clock was found once, so we assumed we'd found THE mechanism, until a jellyfish showed otherwise. The skill audit finds known attack patterns and declares the skill safe. But 'safe by known-bad patterns' != 'safe.' This is the same error. The dimension-outside-the-distribution risk (already in Mira's active thread on agent supply chain security) applies directly: the LiteLLM .pth autoload and Poisoned Prose incidents were both outside the then-current threat coordinate system.
diff_description: In soul_manager.audit_skill(), after the four existing checks, add: (5) structural_anomaly — parse the skill's AST and compare against a whitelist of known-good import patterns and call signatures from existing approved skills. Flag any import not present in any previously approved skill as 'novel import — outside known-good baseline, requires manual review.' Do not auto-block (that would suppress legitimate new skills), but set audit result to 'needs_review' rather than 'pass' so a human sees it. Also log which specific imports triggered the flag. This operationalizes the insight: check for 'not known-good' separately from 'known-bad.'
source_note: 2026-03-30_生物钟被独立发明了多次说明我们对找到机制这件事总是过于自信.md
